design_philosophy.core_loop: Players expand civilization through city founding, technological research, military/diplomatic conquest, and resource management across a tile-based map from 4000 BC to 2020 AD
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing expansion, resource production, scientific advancement, and diplomatic/military competition with rival civilizations for map dominance
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Build and guide a historical civilization from primitive tribe to space-faring superpower through strategic planning and negotiation
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Sid Meier's intentional rebalancing from Civilization 1 to elevate trade and diplomacy as equal paths to victory alongside military conquest; emphasis on player agency through multiple victory conditions and sandbox-style open strategy

difficulty.checkpointing: Game persists indefinitely until player achieves one of three victory conditions or chooses to end; cities permanently lost upon conquest
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects from 21 historical civilizations with distinct traits; generates random maps with custom specifications; can adjust computer opponent intelligence (implied)

progression.upgrade_system: Technology tree progressing through eras from ancient to modern; scientific research unlocks new units, city improvements, and strategic options
progression.unlock_mechanism: Technologies gate access to advanced units and wonders; exploration reveals map and resources; diplomatic contact enables trade routes and alliances
replayability_hooks: 21 distinct civilizations with different traits and historical identities
Procedural map generation with custom parameters
Multiple victory conditions: conquest, spaceship, or survival to 2020 AD
Mod support for custom scenarios and game mechanics
Multiplayer mode (via addon) enables human competition
Different AI behavior across opponent civilizations

world_building_techniques: Historical civilization selection (Americans, Aztecs, Babylonians, etc.) evokes distinct cultural identities
Terrain and resource distribution creates implicit geographic narratives
Wonders of the World serve as landmark achievements
Technology era progression (ancient to modern) structures implicit historical advancement narrative
Diplomatic reputation system adds political world-building
Victory through spaceship completion implies sci-fi endgame narrative

faction_indicators: 21 playable civilizations with distinct identities and historical archetypes
Diplomatic reputation affects NPC civilization behavior and alliance formation
Computer-controlled civilizations act as persistent rival factions with own expansion goals
danger_signals: Diplomatic reputation decline affecting trade and alliances
Barbarian incursions in unexplored or contested territory
environmental_storytelling: Tile improvements (irrigation, roads, mines) show civilization infrastructure development
City placement reflects strategic control of resources and terrain
Wonder construction indicates technological milestones and cultural dominance
Explored vs. unexplored map regions create tension around discovery
Terrain types (mountains, water, forest) constrain and guide expansion patterns
